—Here is a very serious question concerning the character of a citizen of Zion. It is the happiness of glorified saints that they dwell in the holy hill; they are at home there, and will be forever there. It concerns us to make sure we have a place among them. A very plain and particular answer is given here. Those who desire to know their duty will find Scripture a faithful director, and conscience a faithful monitor. A citizen of Zion is sincere in his religion; he is really what he professes to be, and endeavors to stand complete in all the will of God. He is just both to God and man, and when speaking to both, speaks the truth in his heart. He scorns and abhors wrong and fraud; he cannot consider a good bargain or a saving one that is made with a lie; and knows that whoever wrongs his neighbor will prove, in the end, to have most injured himself. He is very careful to do no hurt to anyone. He speaks evil of no man, makes others' faults not the matter of his common talk, makes the best of everyone, and the worst of nobody. If an ill-natured story is told him, he will disprove it if he can; if not, it goes no further. He values men by their virtue and piety. Wicked people are vile, worthless, and good for nothing, as the word signifies. He does not think less of anyone’s piety because of his poverty or low status. He believes that serious piety puts honor on a man more than wealth or a great name. He honors such people, desires their company and an interest in their prayers, is glad to show them respect, or do them a kindness. By this we may judge ourselves in some measure. Even wise and good men may swear to their own hurt; but see how strong the obligation is: a man must rather suffer loss to himself and his family than wrong his neighbor. He will not increase his estate by extortion or bribery. He will not, for any gain or hope of it to himself, do anything to harm a righteous cause. Every true living member of the church, like the church itself, is built upon a Rock. Whoever does these things shall not be moved forever. The grace of God shall always be sufficient for him. The union of these qualities and this conduct can only spring from repentance for sin, faith in the Savior, and love to him. In these respects let us examine and prove ourselves.
